hypothesis: We believe mid-market advertisers (50-500 employees) over-rely on broad demographic targeting because self-serve segmentation tools require technical expertise their teams lack, resulting in wasted impressions and lower ROAS compared to enterprise competitors using sophisticated audience models.

assumptions.statement: Mid-market advertisers are aware their targeting is less sophisticated than enterprise competitors
Broad demographic targeting is a top-3 pain point for mid-market performance teams
Teams lack the SQL or data science skills to build custom audience segments themselves
Existing DSP audience tools (lookalike, interest targeting) don't meet mid-market needs
Advertisers can interpret audience performance reports without a dedicated analyst
CRM or first-party data exists at mid-market scale but isn't connected to ad platforms
Platform APIs expose enough signal to build meaningful custom segments at this scale
Mid-market teams have budget headroom to pay for advanced segmentation above DSP fees
Performance improvement from better segmentation is measurable within a campaign cycle

scripts.raw_text:
1. Tell me about your current audience targeting approach across your campaigns.
2. Don't you think broad demographic targeting is the main reason your ROAS is underperforming?
3. How do you decide which audience segments to use for a new campaign?
4. Would you use an AI-powered segmentation tool if we built one?
5. How often do you review audience performance and adjust your targeting?
6. Have you ever tried to build custom audience segments? What happened?
7. Surely lookalike audiences aren't giving you the granularity you need, right?
8. What would a perfect audience targeting workflow look like for your team?
9. Do you think the problem is your data quality or the platform tools?
10. If we gave you better segmentation tools, would that fix your ROAS issues?

sessions.raw_notes:
P1 â€” Head of Paid Media, e-commerce brand, ~120 employees. Has been in the role 3 years.

Primary channels: Meta Ads Manager and Google DV360. 'We basically live in Meta's targeting UI â€” it's the one interface my whole team is fluent in.'

Audience strategy is interest categories plus lookalike audiences built off customer lists, refreshed quarterly. Described the iOS 14 fallout in detail: 'Our lookalikes have gotten progressively broader every year since iOS 14. The signal quality dropped and we never really recovered. We used to get lookalike audiences that performed 30-40% better than broad. Now it's maybe 10-15% better if we're lucky.'

Tried to build custom segments using Klaviyo CRM data to feed into DV360 but couldn't complete the integration: 'IT said it was too complex to set up without an engineer. We ended up exporting CSVs manually from Klaviyo every quarter and uploading them. It takes half a day and we always miss the campaign window.'

Spends about 4 hours per week on audience management â€” 'mostly those CSV exports and re-uploads. It's tedious but I don't see an alternative.'

Not aware of any mid-market segmentation tools: 'I'd love something that just connected our CRM to our DSP automatically without needing an engineer in the loop every time.'

On budget and approval: 'We have some headroom for tools but my CFO will only approve a renewal if we can show ROAS improvement within 90 days. That's the real constraint.'

Sceptical of AI promises: 'Every vendor claims to be AI now. I need to see the actual targeting logic and the data lineage, not a black box that just says it made things better.'

P2 â€” Digital Marketing Manager, B2B SaaS company, ~80 employees. Sceptical and challenging throughout.

Primary channels: LinkedIn and Google Search, Meta used for retargeting only. Immediately pushed back on the core premise: 'We don't really have a segmentation problem. Our audience is narrow by definition â€” we target CFOs and VPs of Finance at mid-market tech companies. Job title plus company size, that's our entire targeting. Demographics are actually pretty good for us.'

Doesn't feel the pain framed in the hypothesis: 'Broad demographic targeting makes sense for B2C. For us it's not the issue at all. Our ICP is defined by role and industry, not interests or behaviours.'

Does acknowledge one operational annoyance: 'I have to manually update our exclusion lists every month â€” existing customers, churned accounts, people who've already converted. That's annoying and time-consuming, maybe 2 hours a month. But it's not killing our ROAS, it's just boring work.'

Would not pay for a segmentation tool: 'This feels like a product built entirely for B2C advertisers. I'm not your customer.' When pressed on whether any part of it would be useful: 'Look, maybe for the exclusion list automation. But I'd pay maybe $50/month for that, not $500.'

Offered a redirect that was genuinely useful: 'The bigger issue for me isn't targeting at all. It's attribution. I can reach the right people. I just cannot prove it worked. That's where I'd spend money.' This came up unprompted twice.

P3 â€” Performance Marketing Lead, DTC skincare brand, ~200 employees. Strongest pain signal of all sessions.

Runs Meta, TikTok, Pinterest, and YouTube simultaneously. Opened with a specific frustration: 'Every single platform has its own audience builder. If I want the same segment on all four platforms I have to recreate it four times, in four different interfaces, with four different formats. It takes a full Monday morning. That is insane in 2025.'

Tried to use Segment.io as a CDP to centralise audience definitions: 'IT told me it was too complex to implement without a dedicated engineer. We got 3 weeks into the integration and it stalled. The engineer got pulled onto something else and we never finished it. Cost us a lot of time and nothing to show for it.'

Has quantified the performance gap: 'Our ROAS variance between audiences is huge â€” 4x difference between our best and worst segments consistently. I know the opportunity is there. I just don't have the time or tooling to fully capitalise on it.'

CRM data (HubSpot) is good but disconnected: 'We have really clean first-party data in HubSpot. Purchase history, repurchase patterns, product preferences. There is genuinely no clean way to pipe that into TikTok's targeting. I've tried.'

Would pay immediately: 'If you built a tool that syncs one segment definition across all four platforms and keeps it current automatically, I'd pay Â£500/month for that tomorrow. No negotiation. That's a genuine offer.'

One clear dealbreaker: 'It needs to be automatic. If I have to trigger a sync manually every time our CRM data updates, you haven't solved my problem â€” you've just given me a more expensive CSV export.'

P4 â€” VP Marketing, retail brand, ~350 employees. Strategic perspective, less hands-on with day-to-day targeting.

Has a team of three performance marketers, none with data science backgrounds. Acknowledged the gap without much prompting: 'We are definitely leaving money on the table with our targeting. I know it. But it's not the top priority this quarter â€” we have a website replatform consuming most of my team's attention.'

Described an RFM model built in Excel last year: 'One of my team built it. It works in theory but it's completely manual â€” you have to re-run it every month and the outputs aren't connected to anything. Nobody trusts it enough to actually base media decisions on it.'

Has evaluated enterprise solutions and found them inaccessible: 'I've had demos of LiveRamp and Salesforce Audience Studio. Both are genuinely impressive. Both are also priced for companies with a dedicated adtech team and a DMP budget. There is a real market gap between what the platforms offer natively and what enterprise tooling costs.'

Primary blocker is not willingness but compliance: 'Post-GDPR I'm genuinely nervous about tools that touch audience data in novel ways. Before I could buy anything, procurement would want to know about data residency, third-party data sharing agreements, and GDPR compliance certification. That's not a dealbreaker but it's a 3-month procurement process.'

Willing to pay if the ROI case is clear: 'If it saved my team 5 hours a week on audience management, I could build the business case for Â£800-1,000/month easily. The challenge is proving that case within a 90-day trial window.'

P5 â€” Freelance performance marketing consultant, works with 6 mid-market clients across B2C and DTC. Offered the most nuanced and challenging perspective.

Opened by reframing the problem entirely: 'I'd push back on your hypothesis. I'd say about half my clients actually want more sophisticated segmentation â€” and half of them don't trust their own data enough to build on it in the first place. The tooling is not the primary constraint for that second group.'

On manual pain: 'Every single client I have is doing CSV exports and re-uploads to manage audiences. Every one. It's embarrassing that this is still how the industry works. That part of the hypothesis is completely right.'

But challenged the root cause: 'The real problem I see more often isn't the tooling, it's that teams don't have a process for audience hygiene. They keep adding new segments and they never retire the old ones. Over time they're running 40 overlapping audiences and nobody knows which are live or why.'

Data quality as the hard blocker: 'Most of my clients have CRM data but it's messy â€” duplicate records, inconsistent lifecycle stages, email fields that are 30% invalid. You cannot build good segments on top of dirty data, no matter how good the tool is. Any product that wins here needs to handle data cleaning and normalisation as a core feature, not an afterthought.'

On willingness to pay: 'I'd recommend it to my clients if and only if the implementation was genuinely turnkey. Not a 6-week integration project. If you can get them to first value in one day, there's a real market. If it takes a quarter, they'll churn before they see results.'

P6 — VP Programmatic, enterprise consumer goods company, 8,000 employees. Manages a $60M programmatic budget across 12 brands.

Has a full data science team dedicated to audience modeling and a DMP (Adobe Audience Manager) with CRM integration. Also has identity graph access through LiveRamp and runs custom look-alike models in Python.

"We've solved the technical problem of audience segmentation. The problem we haven't solved is getting the models into the buying platforms fast enough to matter. There's a 24-48 hour lag between our model outputs and the audience being live in DV360."

Their data science team builds new audience segments weekly; activating them requires an API call sequence through LiveRamp to DV360 that requires manual intervention when segment populations are under threshold.

"We have 400 active segments across our brands. Managing the lifecycle — refreshing, suppressing, archiving — is a full-time job for two people. The tools don't give you a unified view across brands."

Platform fragmentation is the core operational challenge. Each brand runs independently in Meta, TikTok, Pinterest, and their DSP — audience definitions are inconsistent across platforms.

"We have 'high-value customer' defined 14 different ways across our brand stack. There's no source of truth."

Would not benefit from an SMB audience segmentation tool — already past that stage. What he'd pay for is a segment orchestration layer that manages lifecycle and syncs definitions across platforms. Budget: $10-15K/month at enterprise scale.

Confirming on operational complexity pain. Beyond the scope of most SMB-focused tools in this space.
sessions.summary: Enterprise VP Programmatic with a sophisticated DMP + data science stack who has solved the modeling problem but faces a segment orchestration challenge: 24-48 hour activation lag, inconsistent segment definitions across 14 brand contexts, and a 2-person team managing lifecycle for 400 active segments. Key insight: the enterprise problem is segment operationalization and governance, not creation — a fundamentally different product than mid-market audience building automation.

sessions.raw_notes:
P7 — Director of Performance Marketing, DTC apparel brand, 250 employees. Recently churned from Lotame Spherical (DMP platform) after 18 months.

Churned because Lotame required a pixel implementation on their Shopify store that conflicted with their Consent Management Platform. After 6 months of trying to make it work, they gave up.

"We were paying $4,000/month for a segment tool we couldn't activate because we couldn't legally collect the data it needed."

Now using Meta's native audience tools, Google's Customer Match, and manual cohort analysis in BigQuery. Has accepted that multi-touch is effectively broken for DTC brands post-iOS.

"Honestly, going first-party has been better for us. Our ROAS from Customer Match audiences is 40% higher than our best third-party segment ever was. But we're only reaching 20% of the people we could reach."

The gap: they have good first-party audiences for existing customers but cannot build meaningful prospecting audiences without third-party data.

"We have 50,000 customers. Our look-alike audiences cap out at maybe 2M people. We need scale we can't get from first-party alone."

Would not go back to a third-party DMP. Is evaluating clean room solutions as an alternative — match existing customers to publisher data without moving data.

"That's where I think the future is, but the technology isn't ready for a company our size."

His ideal tool: automated first-party audience building from their Shopify + Klaviyo data, with direct sync to Meta and Google — no DMP middleware. "I'd pay $500-800/month for a Shopify-native tool that automatically builds and syncs custom audiences without me touching the API."

Strongly confirming on audience reach limitation. Challenging on third-party data approaches; bullish on first-party native solutions.
sessions.summary: Director who churned from a $4K/month DMP after a compliance conflict made it unusable — and found that going first-party-only actually improved ROAS by 40%, though it capped audience reach. Provides a clear product spec: Shopify-native first-party audience automation with direct Meta/Google sync at $500-800/month. Key reframe: the first-party constraint is a feature for privacy-forward buyers, not just a limitation.

sessions.raw_notes:
P8 — CFO, mid-market e-commerce company, 180 employees. Approves all martech purchases over $3,000/month.

Asked about marketing's audience segmentation spend for the first time six months ago during an annual planning review. Found that the marketing team had three overlapping tools — a DMP, a CDP, and a data append service — totaling $8,400/month. None of the three teams could clearly explain what problem each solved that the others didn't.

"I consolidated the three tools into one. The marketing team was upset for a month, then admitted they'd been paying for tools that weren't talking to each other anyway."

From a financial perspective, the ROI case for audience segmentation tools is weak in his experience.

"Marketing tells me better segmentation improves ROAS. Maybe. But they can never tell me by how much before we buy, and they can never prove it after. The attribution is always murky."

Would approve spend on a segmentation tool only if it came with a measurement framework built in — a test vs. control design that demonstrates ROAS lift attributable to the segmentation.

"Show me a holdout test that proves you're adding value. That's all I need."

Has a simple heuristic: if a tool cannot be evaluated on a 90-day pilot with a clear success metric, he doesn't approve it.

"If you can't agree upfront on what success looks like, you're not ready to buy."

Neutral on the specific pain (defers to marketing team). Challenging on ROI demonstrability — this is the gating criterion.
sessions.summary: CFO who consolidated three overlapping audience tools ($8,400/month) after finding they weren't integrated. His approval criterion: a built-in measurement framework that proves ROAS lift via holdout testing, agreed upfront. Critical insight: the finance buyer cares about demonstrable ROI more than features, and requires a structured measurement framework as a purchasing prerequisite.

sessions.raw_notes:
P9 — Head of Digital Marketing, beauty retailer, based in Singapore, 400 employees across APAC.

Manages campaigns across Meta, Google, TikTok, Line (Japan and Thailand), and Lazada Sponsored (Southeast Asia). Each platform has a different audience taxonomy and data availability.

"In APAC we're dealing with 8 platforms across 6 markets that each have their own definition of 'interest' and 'behavior'. There is no unified segmentation — it's impossible."

Third-party data quality in Southeast Asia is significantly worse than in the US or EU. Many data providers have poor coverage outside major cities.

"We've tried 4 data providers for look-alike enrichment in Indonesia. All four were garbage — response rates under 0.1%."

Her primary solution: heavy use of first-party CRM data via Customer Match and manual audience creation in each platform separately.

"We upload the same CRM segment to six platforms manually every week. It takes one person half a day."

The manual sync is the core pain. She's tried automated solutions but they typically cover only Meta + Google, not Line or Lazada.

"Every tool covers the US platforms. No one has cracked Southeast Asia distribution."

Would pay for a tool that automates CRM-to-platform sync across all her markets including Line and Lazada, even if the AI/ML layer was basic.

"I don't need sophisticated segmentation. I need a reliable pipe from my CRM to my media platforms."

Confirming on manual sync pain and first-party-only constraint. Challenging on most Western tools having meaningful APAC platform coverage.
sessions.summary: APAC-based marketer coordinating campaigns across 8 platforms in 6 markets where third-party data quality is poor and manual CRM sync to each platform takes half a person's day weekly. Most commercial tools cover only Meta + Google, missing Line, Lazada, and other APAC-specific platforms. Key insight: the APAC market's primary need is reliable CRM-to-platform piping across a fragmented platform landscape — not sophisticated segmentation logic.

sessions.raw_notes:
P10 — Data Analyst (Marketing), SaaS company, 220 employees. Has built a custom audience sync pipeline using Python, dbt, and the Meta/Google APIs.

Spent three months building an automated pipeline: Salesforce CRM data → dbt transforms in BigQuery → Python script that hashes and uploads to Meta Customer Match and Google Customer Match on a weekly schedule.

"It works perfectly for our use case. We have 12 audience segments updating automatically every Sunday night. Monday morning the media team has fresh audiences in both platforms without touching anything."

Current limitations: only covers Meta and Google. LinkedIn, TikTok, and Bing are still manual. The LinkedIn API for audience upload is complex and rate-limited; TikTok's is poorly documented.

"I've tried to extend the pipeline to LinkedIn four times. Their API is a nightmare."

Also doesn't cover suppression logic — ensuring churned customers are excluded from acquisition campaigns.

"We still do suppression manually because it's harder to automate the 'who NOT to target' logic across different platform taxonomies."

His honest assessment of commercial tools: "Most of them are basically doing what I built but with a UI on top. I could replace my pipeline tomorrow for $300-500/month if a tool covered LinkedIn and TikTok natively. That's the only reason I'd switch."

Has a very high technical bar for evaluation — will inspect API docs before agreeing to a trial.

"Don't show me a demo until you've given me your API documentation. I want to know if you're actually handling the platform edge cases or just the happy path."

Strongly confirming on pain (has solved 70% of it himself). High bar for switching — only LinkedIn + TikTok native coverage would make him switch from his custom build.
sessions.summary: Data Analyst who built a custom Python/dbt/API pipeline automating audience syncing to Meta and Google but cannot extend it to LinkedIn (API too complex) or TikTok (poor documentation). Would pay $300-500/month for a commercial tool only if it natively supports LinkedIn and TikTok. Will evaluate tool quality via API docs before agreeing to a demo — highest technical sophistication of all participants.
